item_stem: The government is considering a new tax bracket that applies a higher rate only to annual incomes above $1 million. Which outcome would you prefer?
option_A: Implement the higher tax rate on incomes above $1 million to fund public services.
option_B: Keep the current rates; higher taxes on top earners may reduce investment and job creation.
contrast_note: High earner tax rate vs. investment incentive concern

item_stem: A city is deciding how to fund road repairs. Which approach do you prefer?
option_A: Raise property taxes on higher-value homes so wealthier residents contribute more.
option_B: Charge all drivers a flat toll based on road use regardless of income or property value.
contrast_note: Progressive property tax vs. flat usage fee

item_stem: When a person dies and leaves assets to heirs, some argue those assets should be taxed (an "estate" or "inheritance" tax). What is your preference?
option_A: Large inherited estates should be taxed to limit the concentration of inherited wealth.
option_B: Families should be able to pass on what they earned without government taxation at death.
contrast_note: Estate taxation vs. family wealth transfer

item_stem: A state needs to raise additional revenue. Which of these two options would you support?
option_A: A modest increase in income taxes scaled so higher earners pay a larger percentage.
option_B: A modest increase in the general sales tax applied equally to all purchases.
contrast_note: Progressive income tax vs. flat consumption tax

item_stem: Some economists propose a tax on net financial wealth (such as stocks and savings) held above a high threshold, in addition to taxing income. Which is closer to your view?
option_A: A wealth tax on very large fortunes is a fair way to reduce extreme inequality.
option_B: Taxing accumulated assets on top of income amounts to double taxation and discourages saving.
contrast_note: Wealth tax fairness vs. double taxation concern

item_stem: The federal government is running a budget deficit (spending more than it takes in). What should policymakers prioritize?
option_A: Maintain current spending on social programs even if it means carrying the deficit longer.
option_B: Cut government spending to reduce the deficit even if some programs are scaled back.
contrast_note: Deficit tolerance for programs vs. spending cuts

item_stem: A recession has led to rising unemployment. Which response do you favor?
option_A: Increase government spending on public projects and aid to stimulate the economy.
option_B: Avoid adding to the national debt; let the private sector lead the economic recovery.
contrast_note: Government stimulus vs. private sector recovery

item_stem: A proposal would expand free public transit in major cities funded by the federal government. Which do you prefer?
option_A: Federal funding for expanded public transit is a worthwhile public investment.
option_B: Transit systems should be locally funded or run by private operators not the federal government.
contrast_note: Federal transit investment vs. local/private control

item_stem: The government is reviewing spending on defense versus domestic social programs. If cuts are necessary, which priority do you support?
option_A: Protect social program funding and reduce military spending if cuts are needed.
option_B: Protect defense spending and reduce domestic program spending if cuts are needed.
contrast_note: Social spending priority vs. defense spending priority

item_stem: A proposal would provide government subsidies (direct financial support) to help lower-income families afford housing in expensive cities. What is your preference?
option_A: Government housing subsidies are an important tool for ensuring basic affordability.
option_B: Housing affordability is better addressed by reducing zoning restrictions and increasing private construction.
contrast_note: Direct housing subsidy vs. deregulation and private supply

item_stem: A proposal would raise the national minimum wage significantly above its current level. Which outcome do you prefer?
option_A: Raise the minimum wage to ensure all full-time workers earn a living wage.
option_B: Keep the minimum wage lower; significant increases can lead businesses to cut jobs or hours.
contrast_note: Living wage floor vs. employment effects concern

item_stem: Workers at a company want to form a union (a collective organization that negotiates wages and conditions on their behalf). Which best reflects your view?
option_A: Workers should have strong legal protections to organize and collectively bargain with employers.
option_B: Workers and employers should be free to negotiate individual contracts without mandatory union processes.
contrast_note: Collective bargaining protections vs. individual contract freedom

item_stem: A bill would require employers to provide a minimum of two weeks of paid family leave per year. What is your preference?
option_A: A federal paid leave requirement protects workers who cannot negotiate this benefit on their own.
option_B: Leave policies are better determined by employers and market competition not federal mandates.
contrast_note: Federal leave mandate vs. employer-market determination

item_stem: Some states have "right-to-work" laws which mean workers in a unionized workplace cannot be required to join the union or pay dues. What is your view?
option_A: Right-to-work laws weaken unions and should not be encouraged at the federal level.
option_B: No worker should be compelled to join a union or pay dues as a condition of employment.
contrast_note: Union strength protection vs. individual opt-out right

item_stem: A proposal would limit the ratio between the highest and lowest paid employees within a single company. What is your preference?
option_A: Caps on pay ratios within companies would help reduce extreme income inequality.
option_B: Pay levels within a company should be determined by the market and individual performance not government rules.
contrast_note: Pay ratio cap vs. market-determined compensation

item_stem: A proposal would replace private health insurance with a single government-run insurance program covering all residents (sometimes called "single-payer" or "Medicare for All"). What is your preference?
option_A: A single government insurance program would ensure everyone has coverage regardless of employment or income.
option_B: A competitive private insurance market with government support for those in need is preferable to a government monopoly.
contrast_note: Universal government coverage vs. private market with targeted aid

item_stem: Prescription drug prices in the U.S. are among the highest in the world. Which approach do you prefer?
option_A: Allow the government to directly negotiate drug prices with pharmaceutical companies.
option_B: Encourage more competition among drug manufacturers rather than having the government set prices.
contrast_note: Government price negotiation vs. market competition

item_stem: A proposal would make public four-year universities tuition-free for all students funded by the federal government. Which best reflects your view?
option_A: Free public college would expand opportunity and reduce the burden of student debt.
option_B: Tuition-free college for all is an inefficient subsidy; aid should be targeted to those who cannot afford it.
contrast_note: Universal free tuition vs. means-tested aid

item_stem: Should the government provide free broadband internet access in underserved rural and low-income areas?
option_A: Internet access is a basic utility and the government should ensure universal access.
option_B: Private providers with incentive-based contracts can extend service more efficiently than government programs.
contrast_note: Public utility model vs. private incentive model

item_stem: A town is deciding whether to expand its public library system or offer vouchers residents can use at private libraries. Which do you prefer?
option_A: Expand the publicly funded library system to serve the whole community.
option_B: Vouchers for private libraries give residents more choice and may be more cost-effective.
contrast_note: Public institution expansion vs. voucher and private choice

item_stem: Several large technology companies control the dominant platforms for online search, social media, and e-commerce. What is your preference?
option_A: The government should break up or impose strict rules on dominant tech companies to protect competition.
option_B: Large tech companies succeed because they provide value; heavy regulation risks harming innovation.
contrast_note: Antitrust intervention vs. innovation protection

item_stem: A proposal would require companies above a certain size to include workers on their board of directors (a practice called co-determination). What is your view?
option_A: Workers should have a formal voice in major company decisions not just shareholders.
option_B: Board composition should be determined by shareholders who bear the financial risk of the company.
contrast_note: Worker board representation vs. shareholder governance

item_stem: A new environmental regulation would require factories to meet stricter pollution limits increasing their operating costs. What is your preference?
option_A: Stronger environmental regulations are justified even if they raise costs for businesses.
option_B: Regulations should balance environmental goals with the economic burden placed on businesses.
contrast_note: Environmental standards vs. cost-burden balance

item_stem: A bill would require large corporations to publicly disclose detailed information about their executive pay, tax payments, and lobbying expenditures. What is your preference?
option_A: Greater public disclosure of corporate practices is in the public interest.
option_B: Extensive mandatory disclosure requirements create compliance burdens and may not produce meaningful benefits.
contrast_note: Mandatory transparency vs. compliance burden concern

item_stem: Some argue that financial institutions (banks and investment firms) should be prohibited from certain risky activities to prevent future economic crises. What is your preference?
option_A: Financial institutions should face strict activity restrictions to protect the broader economy.
option_B: Financial firms should largely self-regulate with minimal restrictions; excessive rules slow economic growth.
contrast_note: Activity restrictions for stability vs. self-regulation for growth

item_stem: A tariff is a tax placed on imported goods. A proposal would raise tariffs on foreign manufactured goods to protect domestic factory jobs. What is your preference?
option_A: Tariffs are justified if they protect American workers from unfair foreign competition.
option_B: Tariffs raise prices for consumers and invite retaliation; free trade produces better outcomes overall.
contrast_note: Worker protection via tariffs vs. consumer cost and free trade

item_stem: A trade agreement would lower tariffs between the U.S. and several foreign countries increasing imports and exports. Which outcome do you prefer?
option_A: Trade agreements should be conditioned on labor and environmental protections in partner countries.
option_B: Lower tariffs and freer trade generally benefit both the U.S. economy and partner countries and should be pursued broadly.
contrast_note: Conditional trade with standards vs. broad free trade pursuit

item_stem: A foreign company wants to acquire a major U.S. manufacturer. What should happen?
option_A: The government should carefully screen and potentially block foreign acquisitions of strategic industries.
option_B: Foreign investment in U.S. companies generally benefits the economy and should face minimal restrictions.
contrast_note: Strategic industry protection vs. open foreign investment

item_stem: A bill would require the federal government to purchase only U.S.-made goods and materials for public projects (a "Buy American" requirement). What is your preference?
option_A: Buy American requirements support domestic jobs and industries that matter to national security.
option_B: Government procurement should seek the best value even if that means purchasing from foreign suppliers.
contrast_note: Domestic procurement mandate vs. best-value open procurement

item_stem: Some propose providing retraining assistance and income support specifically to workers who lose their jobs due to import competition. What is your view?
option_A: Targeted government support for trade-displaced workers is a fair use of public funds.
option_B: Job displacement assistance should not be tied to trade; general unemployment insurance is more equitable.
contrast_note: Trade-specific worker aid vs. general unemployment insurance
